mnemonics: Closed + Bounded = Compact (Heine-Borel Theorem in R^n)
Sequentially Compact: Every sequence has a convergent subsequence
Totally Bounded: Can cover with finitely many ε-balls
keyPoints: A subset of a metric space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover.
In R^n with the standard metric, a set is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
Compactness implies sequential compactness and the Bolzano-Weierstrass property in metric spaces.
commonMistakes: Assuming 'closed and bounded' implies compactness in every metric space (it's only true in R^n and a few others).
Confusing completeness (Cauchy sequences converge) with compactness (which is a stronger property).
Thinking the continuous image of a closed set is always closed (it's the image of a compact set that is compact).
